question:
B-2. Suppose that \( G \) is a group generated by elements \( A \) and \( B \), that is, every element of \( G \) can be written as a finite "word" \( A^{n_{1}} B^{n_{2}} A^{n_{1}} \cdots B^{n_{k}} \), where \( n_{1}, \ldots, n_{k} \) are any integers, and \( A^{0}=B^{n}=1 \) as usual. Also, suppose that \( A^{4}=B^{7}=A B A^{-1} B=1, A^{2} \neq 1 \), and \( B \neq 1 \).
(a) How many elements of \( G \) are of the form \( C^{2} \) with \( C \) in \( G \) ?
(b) Write each such square as a word in \( A \) and \( B \).
solution:
B-2.
The answers are (a) 8 ; (b) \( 1, A^{2}, B, B^{2}, B^{3}, B^{4}, B^{5}, B^{6} \). Since \( B=\left(B^{4}\right)^{2}, B^{3}=\left(B^{5}\right)^{2}, B^{5}=\left(B^{6}\right)^{2} \), the elements in the answer to (b) are all squares in \( G \). They are distinct since \( B \) has order 7 and \( A \) has order 4. To show that there are no other squares, we first note that \( A B A^{-1} B=1 \) implies \( A B=B^{-1} A \). Then
\[
A B^{2}=\left(B^{-1} A\right) B=B^{-1}(A B)=B^{-1}\left(B^{-1} A\right)=B^{-2} A .
\]

Similarly \( A B^{n}=B^{-n} A \) for the other \( n \) 's in \( \{0,1, \ldots, 6\} \) and so for all integers \( n \). With this, one obtains
\[
\left(B^{\prime} A^{\prime}\right)\left(B^{h} A^{k}\right)=B^{u} A^{v} \text { with } u=i+(-1)^{\prime} h, v=j+k .
\]

Thus the set \( S \) of elements of the form \( B^{\prime} A^{\prime} \) is closed under multiplication. \( S \) is finite since \( i \) and \( j \) may be restricted to \( 0 \leqq i \leqq 6 \) and \( 0 \leqq j \leqq 3 \). Hence \( S \) is a group and so \( S=G \). It then follows from (P) that the squares in \( G \) are the \( B^{\prime \prime} A^{v} \) with \( u=i\left[1+(-1)^{\prime}\right] \) and \( v=2 j \). If \( j \) is odd, \( u=0 \) and \( v \equiv 2(\bmod 4) \). If \( j \) is even, \( v \equiv 0(\bmod 4) \). Thus there are no squares other than those listed above.

descriptive_long.question:
B-2. Suppose that \( groupg \) is a group generated by elements \( generatora \) and \( generatorb \), that is, every element of \( groupg \) can be written as a finite "word" \( generatora^{firstint} generatorb^{secondint} generatora^{firstint} \cdots generatorb^{intvar_{wordcount}} \), where \( firstint, \ldots , intvar_{wordcount} \) are any integers, and \( generatora^{0}=generatorb^{intvar}=1 \) as usual. Also, suppose that \( generatora^{4}=generatorb^{7}=generatora generatorb generatora^{-1} generatorb=1, generatora^{2} \neq 1 \), and \( generatorb \neq 1 \).
(a) How many elements of \( groupg \) are of the form \( elementc^{2} \) with \( elementc \) in \( groupg \) ?
(b) Write each such square as a word in \( generatora \) and \( generatorb \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2.
The answers are (a) 8 ; (b) \( 1, generatora^{2}, generatorb, generatorb^{2}, generatorb^{3}, generatorb^{4}, generatorb^{5}, generatorb^{6} \). Since \( generatorb=\left(generatorb^{4}\right)^{2},\; generatorb^{3}=\left(generatorb^{5}\right)^{2},\; generatorb^{5}=\left(generatorb^{6}\right)^{2} \), the elements in the answer to (b) are all squares in \( groupg \). They are distinct since \( generatorb \) has order 7 and \( generatora \) has order 4. To show that there are no other squares, we first note that \( generatora\,generatorb\,generatora^{-1}\,generatorb=1 \) implies \( generatora\,generatorb=generatorb^{-1}\,generatora \). Then
\[
generatora\,generatorb^{2}=\left(generatorb^{-1}\,generatora\right)generatorb=generatorb^{-1}(generatora\,generatorb)=generatorb^{-1}\left(generatorb^{-1}\,generatora\right)=generatorb^{-2}\,generatora .
\]
Similarly \( generatora\,generatorb^{intvar}=generatorb^{-intvar}\,generatora \) for the other \( intvar \)'s in \( \{0,1, \ldots , 6\} \) and so for all integers \( intvar \). With this, one obtains
\[
\left(generatorb^{exponenti}\,generatora^{exponentj}\right)\left(generatorb^{exponenth}\,generatora^{wordcount}\right)=generatorb^{exponentu}\,generatora^{exponentv} \text { with } exponentu=exponenti+(-1)^{exponentj}\,exponenth,\quad exponentv=exponentj+wordcount .
\]
Thus the set \( subgroup \) of elements of the form \( generatorb^{exponenti}\,generatora^{exponentj} \) is closed under multiplication. \( subgroup \) is finite since \( exponenti \) and \( exponentj \) may be restricted to \( 0 \leqq exponenti \leqq 6 \) and \( 0 \leqq exponentj \leqq 3 \). Hence \( subgroup \) is a group and so \( subgroup=groupg \). It then follows from (propertyp) that the squares in \( groupg \) are the \( generatorb^{\prime \prime}\,generatora^{exponentv} \) with \( exponentu=exponenti\left[1+(-1)^{exponentj}\right] \) and \( exponentv=2\,exponentj \). If \( exponentj \) is odd, \( exponentu=0 \) and \( exponentv \equiv 2(\bmod 4) \). If \( exponentj \) is even, \( exponentv \equiv 0(\bmod 4) \). Thus there are no squares other than those listed above.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that \( cheeseboard \) is a group generated by elements \( sunflower \) and \( watermelon \), that is, every element of \( cheeseboard \) can be written as a finite "word" \( sunflower^{sandcastle} watermelon^{raincloud} sunflower^{sandcastle} \cdots watermelon^{hummingbird} \), where \( sandcastle, \ldots, hummingbird \) are any integers, and \( sunflower^{0}=watermelon^{lighthouse}=1 \) as usual. Also, suppose that \( sunflower^{4}=watermelon^{7}=sunflower watermelon sunflower^{-1} watermelon=1, sunflower^{2} \neq 1 \), and \( watermelon \neq 1 \).
(a) How many elements of \( cheeseboard \) are of the form \( buttercup^{2} \) with \( buttercup \) in \( cheeseboard \) ?
(b) Write each such square as a word in \( sunflower \) and \( watermelon \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The answers are (a) 8 ; (b) \( 1, sunflower^{2}, watermelon, watermelon^{2}, watermelon^{3}, watermelon^{4}, watermelon^{5}, watermelon^{6} \). Since \( watermelon=\left(watermelon^{4}\right)^{2}, watermelon^{3}=\left(watermelon^{5}\right)^{2}, watermelon^{5}=\left(watermelon^{6}\right)^{2} \), the elements in the answer to (b) are all squares in \( cheeseboard \). They are distinct since \( watermelon \) has order 7 and \( sunflower \) has order 4. To show that there are no other squares, we first note that \( sunflower watermelon sunflower^{-1} watermelon=1 \) implies \( sunflower watermelon=watermelon^{-1} sunflower \). Then
\[
sunflower watermelon^{2}=\left(watermelon^{-1} sunflower\right) watermelon=watermelon^{-1}(sunflower watermelon)=watermelon^{-1}\left(watermelon^{-1} sunflower\right)=watermelon^{-2} sunflower .
\]
Similarly \( sunflower watermelon^{lighthouse}=watermelon^{-lighthouse} sunflower \) for the other \( lighthouse \)'s in \( \{0,1, \ldots, 6\} \) and so for all integers \( lighthouse \). With this, one obtains
\[
\left(watermelon^{daydream} sunflower^{paintbrush}\right)\left(watermelon^{bridgework} sunflower^{hummingbird}\right)=watermelon^{starlight} sunflower^{moonstone} \text { with } starlight=daydream+(-1)^{paintbrush} bridgework, moonstone=paintbrush+hummingbird .
\]
Thus the set \( dragonfly \) of elements of the form \( watermelon^{daydream} sunflower^{paintbrush} \) is closed under multiplication. \( dragonfly \) is finite since \( daydream \) and \( paintbrush \) may be restricted to \( 0 \leqq daydream \leqq 6 \) and \( 0 \leqq paintbrush \leqq 3 \). Hence \( dragonfly \) is a group and so \( dragonfly=cheeseboard \). It then follows from (parchment) that the squares in \( cheeseboard \) are the \( watermelon^{starlight} sunflower^{moonstone} \) with \( starlight=daydream\left[1+(-1)^{paintbrush}\right] \) and \( moonstone=2 paintbrush \). If \( paintbrush \) is odd, \( starlight=0 \) and \( moonstone \equiv 2(\bmod 4) \). If \( paintbrush \) is even, \( moonstone \equiv 0(\bmod 4) \). Thus there are no squares other than those listed above.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-2. Suppose that \( chaosset \) is a group generated by elements \( negating \) and \( affirming \), that is, every element of \( chaosset \) can be written as a finite "word" \( negating^{fractionalfirst} affirming^{fractionalsecond} negating^{fractionalfirst} \cdots affirming^{irrational_{boundless}} \), where \( fractionalfirst, \ldots, irrational_{boundless} \) are any integers, and \( negating^{0}=affirming^{irrational}=1 \) as usual. Also, suppose that \( negating^{4}=affirming^{7}=negating affirming negating^{-1} affirming=1, negating^{2} \neq 1 \), and \( affirming \neq 1 \).
(a) How many elements of \( chaosset \) are of the form \( variableelem^{2} \) with \( variableelem \) in \( chaosset \) ?
(b) Write each such square as a word in \( negating \) and \( affirming \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-2.
The answers are (a) 8 ; (b) \( 1, negating^{2}, affirming, affirming^{2}, affirming^{3}, affirming^{4}, affirming^{5}, affirming^{6} \). Since \( affirming=\left(affirming^{4}\right)^{2}, affirming^{3}=\left(affirming^{5}\right)^{2}, affirming^{5}=\left(affirming^{6}\right)^{2} \), the elements in the answer to (b) are all squares in \( chaosset \). They are distinct since \( affirming \) has order 7 and \( negating \) has order 4. To show that there are no other squares, we first note that \( negating affirming negating^{-1} affirming=1 \) implies \( negating affirming=affirming^{-1} negating \). Then
\[
negating affirming^{2}=\left(affirming^{-1} negating\right) affirming=affirming^{-1}(negating affirming)=affirming^{-1}\left(affirming^{-1} negating\right)=affirming^{-2} negating .
\]

Similarly \( negating affirming^{irrational}=affirming^{-\irrational} negating \) for the other \( irrational \)'s in \( \{0,1, \ldots, 6\} \) and so for all integers \( irrational \). With this, one obtains
\[
\left(affirming^{\prime} negating^{\prime}\right)\left(affirming^{deepness} negating^{boundless}\right)=affirming^{knownness} negating^{scalarvalue} \text { with } knownness=realistic+(-1)^{\prime} deepness, scalarvalue=solidness+boundless .
\]

Thus the set \( endlessset \) of elements of the form \( affirming^{\prime} negating^{\prime} \) is closed under multiplication. \( endlessset \) is finite since \( realistic \) and \( solidness \) may be restricted to \( 0 \leqq realistic \leqq 6 \) and \( 0 \leqq solidness \leqq 3 \). Hence \( endlessset \) is a group and so \( endlessset=chaosset \). It then follows from (misreason) that the squares in \( chaosset \) are the \( affirming^{\prime \prime} negating^{scalarvalue} \) with \( knownness=realistic\left[1+(-1)^{\prime}\right] \) and \( scalarvalue=2 solidness \). If \( solidness \) is odd, \( knownness=0 \) and \( scalarvalue \equiv 2(\bmod 4) \). If \( solidness \) is even, \( scalarvalue \equiv 0(\bmod 4) \). Thus there are no squares other than those listed above.

garbled_string.question:
B-2. Suppose that \( zxiwybrnm \) is a group generated by elements \( plmdqrexc \) and \( sjkhtvopa \), that is, every element of \( zxiwybrnm \) can be written as a finite "word" \( plmdqrexc^{ldfugzoaie} sjkhtvopa^{rmpqbncku} plmdqrexc^{ldfugzoaie} \cdots sjkhtvopa^{yptkmdsvo_{hqwznsote}} \), where \( ldfugzoaie, \ldots, yptkmdsvo_{hqwznsote} \) are any integers, and \( plmdqrexc^{0}=sjkhtvopa^{yptkmdsvo}=1 \) as usual. Also, suppose that \( plmdqrexc^{4}=sjkhtvopa^{7}=plmdqrexc sjkhtvopa plmdqrexc^{-1} sjkhtvopa=1, plmdqrexc^{2} \neq 1 \), and \( sjkhtvopa \neq 1 \).
(a) How many elements of \( zxiwybrnm \) are of the form \( wqtrlyeps^{2} \) with \( wqtrlyeps \) in \( zxiwybrnm \) ?
(b) Write each such square as a word in \( plmdqrexc \) and \( sjkhtvopa \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-2.
The answers are (a) 8 ; (b) \( 1, plmdqrexc^{2}, sjkhtvopa, sjkhtvopa^{2}, sjkhtvopa^{3}, sjkhtvopa^{4}, sjkhtvopa^{5}, sjkhtvopa^{6} \). Since \( sjkhtvopa=\left(sjkhtvopa^{4}\right)^{2}, sjkhtvopa^{3}=\left(sjkhtvopa^{5}\right)^{2}, sjkhtvopa^{5}=\left(sjkhtvopa^{6}\right)^{2} \), the elements in the answer to (b) are all squares in \( zxiwybrnm \). They are distinct since \( sjkhtvopa \) has order 7 and \( plmdqrexc \) has order 4. To show that there are no other squares, we first note that \( plmdqrexc sjkhtvopa plmdqrexc^{-1} sjkhtvopa=1 \) implies \( plmdqrexc sjkhtvopa=sjkhtvopa^{-1} plmdqrexc \). Then
\[
plmdqrexc sjkhtvopa^{2}=\left(sjkhtvopa^{-1} plmdqrexc\right) sjkhtvopa=sjkhtvopa^{-1}(plmdqrexc sjkhtvopa)=sjkhtvopa^{-1}\left(sjkhtvopa^{-1} plmdqrexc\right)=sjkhtvopa^{-2} plmdqrexc .
\]

Similarly \( plmdqrexc sjkhtvopa^{yptkmdsvo}=sjkhtvopa^{-yptkmdsvo} plmdqrexc \) for the other \( yptkmdsvo \) 's in \( \{0,1, \ldots, 6\} \) and so for all integers \( yptkmdsvo \). With this, one obtains
\[
\left(sjkhtvopa^{\prime} plmdqrexc^{\prime}\right)\left(sjkhtvopa^{vcaypldex} plmdqrexc^{hqwznsote}\right)=sjkhtvopa^{tskipvaer} plmdqrexc^{nikjdubza} \text { with } tskipvaer=eglhmqrat+(-1)^{\prime} vcaypldex, nikjdubza=fbzxowrni+hqwznsote .
\]

Thus the set \( qudajblfe \) of elements of the form \( sjkhtvopa^{\prime} plmdqrexc^{\prime} \) is closed under multiplication. \( qudajblfe \) is finite since \( eglhmqrat \) and \( fbzxowrni \) may be restricted to \( 0 \leqq eglhmqrat \leqq 6 \) and \( 0 \leqq fbzxowrni \leqq 3 \). Hence \( qudajblfe \) is a group and so \( qudajblfe=zxiwybrnm \). It then follows from (otniwcrsg) that the squares in \( zxiwybrnm \) are the \( sjkhtvopa^{\prime \prime} plmdqrexc^{nikjdubza} \) with \( tskipvaer=eglhmqrat\left[1+(-1)^{\prime}\right] \) and \( nikjdubza=2 fbzxowrni \). If \( fbzxowrni \) is odd, \( tskipvaer=0 \) and \( nikjdubza \equiv 2(\bmod 4) \). If \( fbzxowrni \) is even, \( nikjdubza \equiv 0(\bmod 4) \). Thus there are no squares other than those listed above.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
G=\langle\,A,B\mid A^{30}=1,\;B^{31}=1,\;A B A^{-1}=B^{11},\;
A^{15}\neq1,\;B\neq1\rangle .
\]

(Because \(11\) is a primitive root modulo \(31\), conjugation by \(A\) induces on
\(\langle B\rangle\cong\mathbf Z_{31}\) the automorphism
\(\varphi(x)=x^{11}\); hence \(G\cong\mathbf Z_{31}\rtimes_{\varphi}\mathbf Z_{30}\).)

a)  Prove that every element of \(G\) can be written uniquely in the normal form  
\[
A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\qquad(0\le i<30,\;0\le j<31).
\]

b)  Let 
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)=\{\,g^{2}\mid g\in G\,\}.
\]
Determine \(|\mathrm{Sq}(G)|\).

c)  Let 
\[
\mathrm{Cu}(G)=\{\,g^{3}\mid g\in G\,\}.
\]
Determine \(|\mathrm{Cu}(G)|\).

d)  Describe explicitly, as words in \(A\) and \(B\), all elements that lie in
\(\mathrm{Sq}(G)\cap\mathrm{Cu}(G)\).
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, exponents of \(A\) are taken modulo \(30\) and those of \(B\) modulo \(31\).

Write  
\[
\varphi(x)=x^{11}\in\operatorname{Aut}\!\bigl(\mathbf Z_{31}\bigr) ,
\qquad 
\varphi^{30}=\operatorname{id}
\]
because \(11^{30}\equiv1\pmod{31}\).

--------------------------------------------------
a)  Normal form and its uniqueness
--------------------------------------------------
From the defining relation \(A B A^{-1}=B^{11}\) we get for every \(i\ge0\)
\[
A^{i} B A^{-i}=B^{11^{\,i}}.
\]
Hence
\[
B\,A^{\,i}=A^{\,i}B^{\,11^{\,i}}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
B^{\,j}A^{\,i}=A^{\,i}B^{\,j\,11^{\,i}},\qquad
0\le i<30,\;0\le j<31. \tag{1}
\]

Formula (1) allows any word to be rewritten with all \(A\)-powers on the
left and all \(B\)-powers on the right, i.e. in the form \(A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\).

Uniqueness.
Assume \(A^{\,i}B^{\,j}=A^{\,i'}B^{\,j'}\).
Then
\[
A^{\,i-i'}=B^{\,j'-j}.
\]
The left-hand side lies in 
\(\langle A\rangle\cong\mathbf Z_{30}\) and the right-hand side in
\(\langle B\rangle\cong\mathbf Z_{31}\).
Because \(\gcd(30,31)=1\), their intersection is trivial, so
\(i\equiv i' \pmod{30}\) and \(j\equiv j'\pmod{31}\).  The normal form is thus unique.

--------------------------------------------------
b)  Counting the squares
--------------------------------------------------
Let \(g=A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\).  Using (1) once,
\[
g^{2}=A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\,A^{\,i}B^{\,j}=A^{\,2i}\,B^{\,j\bigl(1+11^{\,i}\bigr)}. \tag{2}
\]
Put  
\[
s(i)=2i\pmod{30},\qquad c(i)=1+11^{\,i}\pmod{31}. \tag{3}
\]
Formula (2) shows
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,s(i)}B^{\,c(i)j}\;\bigm|\;i\in\mathbf Z_{30},\,j\in\mathbf Z_{31}\bigr\}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 1.  Zeros of \(c(i)\).
\[
c(i)=0\iff 11^{\,i}\equiv-1\pmod{31}.
\]
Because \(11\) has order \(30\) modulo \(31\), this happens exactly for
\(i\equiv15\).

Step 2.  Image sizes.
If \(i=15\) then \(c(i)=0\) and (2) gives \(g^{2}=A^{30}=1\) for every \(j\).
If \(i\neq15\) then \(c(i)\neq0\); multiplication by \(c(i)\) acts
bijectively on \(\mathbf Z_{31}\), so varying \(j\) produces all
\(31\) exponents of \(B\).

Step 3.  Identifying equal \(A\)-exponents.
Because \(\gcd(2,30)=2\), the map \(i\mapsto s(i)=2i\) hits the \(15\) even
residues of \(\mathbf Z_{30}\); each even residue \(s\) has two preimages \(i\)
and \(i+15\).
For \(s=0\) the two preimages are \(i=0\) and \(i=15\).
The case \(i=15\) contributes only the identity, which is already produced
when \(i=0\) and \(j=0\).
Every other even residue \(s\) arises from two indices \(i\) whose
\(c(i)\) are both non-zero, hence lead to the same set
\(\{\,A^{\,s}B^{\,k}\mid k\in\mathbf Z_{31}\}\).

Therefore  
\[
|\mathrm{Sq}(G)|=15\times31=465. \tag{5}
\]

Explicitly,
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,2r}B^{\,k}\;\bigm|\;0\le r<15,\;0\le k<31\bigr\}. \tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------
c)  Counting the cubes
--------------------------------------------------
With the same notation,
\[
g^{3}=A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\,A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\,A^{\,i}B^{\,j}
       =A^{\,3i}\,B^{\,j\bigl(1+11^{\,i}+11^{\,2i}\bigr)}. \tag{7}
\]
Put  
\[
t(i)=3i\pmod{30},\qquad d(i)=1+11^{\,i}+11^{\,2i}\pmod{31}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 1.  Zeros of \(d(i)\).
Set \(x=11^{\,i}\).
Then \(d(i)=0\iff 1+x+x^{2}=0\iff x^{3}=1\) and \(x\neq1\).
In the cyclic group \((\mathbf Z_{31})^{\times}\) of order \(30\)
there are exactly two elements of order \(3\),
namely \(11^{10}\) and \(11^{20}\); hence
\[
d(i)=0\iff i\equiv10,20\pmod{30}. \tag{9}
\]

Step 2.  Non-zero \(d(i)\).
For the remaining \(28\) indices \(d(i)\neq0\); varying \(j\) runs through all \(31\) exponents of \(B\).

Step 3.  Identifying equal \(A\)-exponents.
The map \(i\mapsto t(i)=3i\) has kernel \(\{0,10,20\}\) and image the ten
residues \(0,3,6,\dots,27\).  Each residue occurs for three different
\(i\)'s, and at least one of them satisfies \(d(i)\neq0\).
Thus every coset \(\{\,A^{\,t}B^{\,k}\mid k\}\) with
\(t\equiv0,3,6,\dots,27\) appears exactly once.

Consequently  
\[
|\mathrm{Cu}(G)|=10\times31=310. \tag{10}
\]

Explicitly,
\[
\mathrm{Cu}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,3r}B^{\,k}\;\bigm|\;0\le r<10,\;0\le k<31\bigr\}. \tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------
d)  Squares that are also cubes
--------------------------------------------------
An element \(A^{\,\ell}B^{\,k}\) lies in \(\mathrm{Sq}(G)\) iff \(\ell\) is even,
and in \(\mathrm{Cu}(G)\) iff \(\ell\equiv0\pmod{3}\).
Therefore
\[
\ell\equiv0\pmod{2},\quad\ell\equiv0\pmod{3}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\ell\equiv0\pmod{6}.
\]
Within \(0\le\ell<30\) this gives
\[
\ell\in\{0,6,12,18,24\}. \tag{12}
\]
For each such \(\ell\) the descriptions (6) and (11) both provide every
exponent of \(B\), so for every \(k\) there exist elements whose square and whose cube equal \(A^{\,\ell}B^{\,k}\).
Hence
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)\cap\mathrm{Cu}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,\ell}B^{\,k}\;\bigm|\;
\ell\in\{0,6,12,18,24\},\;0\le k<31\bigr\}, \tag{13}
\]
and
\[
|\mathrm{Sq}(G)\cap\mathrm{Cu}(G)|=5\times31=155. \tag{14}
\]

--------------------------------------------------
Summary
--------------------------------------------------
1.  Unique normal form \(A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\) with \((i,j)\in[0,29]\times[0,30]\).  
2.  Number of squares:  \(|\mathrm{Sq}(G)|=465\).  
3.  Number of cubes:    \(|\mathrm{Cu}(G)|=310\).  
4.  Simultaneous squares and cubes: \(155\) elements, explicitly listed above.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-order relations.  The exponents 30 and 31 and the primitive-root
   conjugation A B A^{-1}=B^{11} force use of cyclic-group automorphisms
   of order 30, far beyond the inversion relation of the original problems.

2. Multiple interacting concepts.  Solving parts (b)–(d) requires
   simultaneous control of three ingredients:
   • semidirect-product normal forms,
   • arithmetic in (ℤ/31ℤ)^×, including primitive roots and roots of unity,
   • counting images of non-trivial endomorphisms of finite abelian groups.

3. Deeper theoretical tools.  One must exploit properties of cyclic groups,
   orders of automorphisms, geometric-series identities in group rings, and
   Chinese-remainder–type counting (e.g. lcm considerations to intersect the
   square and cube sets).

4. Substantially longer solution path.  Where the original problem needed
   only a single conjugation identity, this variant demands four main steps:
   establishing normal form, analysing squares, analysing cubes, and then
   intersecting the two large sets, each step involving non-trivial number-theoretic
   calculations.

5. Much larger search space.  The group now has 930 elements (vs. 66 or 132
   in the originals), and the answer sets (465, 310, 155 elements) cannot be
   listed by inspection; a structural approach is indispensable.

These additions create a kernel variant that is markedly more intricate and
conceptually demanding than both the original and the current kernel versions.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
G=\langle\,A,B\mid A^{30}=1,\;B^{31}=1,\;A B A^{-1}=B^{11},\;
A^{15}\neq1,\;B\neq1\rangle .
\]

(Because \(11\) is a primitive root modulo \(31\), conjugation by \(A\) induces on
\(\langle B\rangle\cong\mathbf Z_{31}\) the automorphism
\(\varphi(x)=x^{11}\); hence \(G\cong\mathbf Z_{31}\rtimes_{\varphi}\mathbf Z_{30}\).)

a)  Prove that every element of \(G\) can be written uniquely in the normal form  
\[
A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\qquad(0\le i<30,\;0\le j<31).
\]

b)  Let 
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)=\{\,g^{2}\mid g\in G\,\}.
\]
Determine \(|\mathrm{Sq}(G)|\).

c)  Let 
\[
\mathrm{Cu}(G)=\{\,g^{3}\mid g\in G\,\}.
\]
Determine \(|\mathrm{Cu}(G)|\).

d)  Describe explicitly, as words in \(A\) and \(B\), all elements that lie in
\(\mathrm{Sq}(G)\cap\mathrm{Cu}(G)\).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, exponents of \(A\) are taken modulo \(30\) and those of \(B\) modulo \(31\).

Write  
\[
\varphi(x)=x^{11}\in\operatorname{Aut}\!\bigl(\mathbf Z_{31}\bigr) ,
\qquad 
\varphi^{30}=\operatorname{id}
\]
because \(11^{30}\equiv1\pmod{31}\).

--------------------------------------------------
a)  Normal form and its uniqueness
--------------------------------------------------
From the defining relation \(A B A^{-1}=B^{11}\) we get for every \(i\ge0\)
\[
A^{i} B A^{-i}=B^{11^{\,i}}.
\]
Hence
\[
B\,A^{\,i}=A^{\,i}B^{\,11^{\,i}}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
B^{\,j}A^{\,i}=A^{\,i}B^{\,j\,11^{\,i}},\qquad
0\le i<30,\;0\le j<31. \tag{1}
\]

Formula (1) allows any word to be rewritten with all \(A\)-powers on the
left and all \(B\)-powers on the right, i.e. in the form \(A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\).

Uniqueness.
Assume \(A^{\,i}B^{\,j}=A^{\,i'}B^{\,j'}\).
Then
\[
A^{\,i-i'}=B^{\,j'-j}.
\]
The left-hand side lies in 
\(\langle A\rangle\cong\mathbf Z_{30}\) and the right-hand side in
\(\langle B\rangle\cong\mathbf Z_{31}\).
Because \(\gcd(30,31)=1\), their intersection is trivial, so
\(i\equiv i' \pmod{30}\) and \(j\equiv j'\pmod{31}\).  The normal form is thus unique.

--------------------------------------------------
b)  Counting the squares
--------------------------------------------------
Let \(g=A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\).  Using (1) once,
\[
g^{2}=A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\,A^{\,i}B^{\,j}=A^{\,2i}\,B^{\,j\bigl(1+11^{\,i}\bigr)}. \tag{2}
\]
Put  
\[
s(i)=2i\pmod{30},\qquad c(i)=1+11^{\,i}\pmod{31}. \tag{3}
\]
Formula (2) shows
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,s(i)}B^{\,c(i)j}\;\bigm|\;i\in\mathbf Z_{30},\,j\in\mathbf Z_{31}\bigr\}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 1.  Zeros of \(c(i)\).
\[
c(i)=0\iff 11^{\,i}\equiv-1\pmod{31}.
\]
Because \(11\) has order \(30\) modulo \(31\), this happens exactly for
\(i\equiv15\).

Step 2.  Image sizes.
If \(i=15\) then \(c(i)=0\) and (2) gives \(g^{2}=A^{30}=1\) for every \(j\).
If \(i\neq15\) then \(c(i)\neq0\); multiplication by \(c(i)\) acts
bijectively on \(\mathbf Z_{31}\), so varying \(j\) produces all
\(31\) exponents of \(B\).

Step 3.  Identifying equal \(A\)-exponents.
Because \(\gcd(2,30)=2\), the map \(i\mapsto s(i)=2i\) hits the \(15\) even
residues of \(\mathbf Z_{30}\); each even residue \(s\) has two preimages \(i\)
and \(i+15\).
For \(s=0\) the two preimages are \(i=0\) and \(i=15\).
The case \(i=15\) contributes only the identity, which is already produced
when \(i=0\) and \(j=0\).
Every other even residue \(s\) arises from two indices \(i\) whose
\(c(i)\) are both non-zero, hence lead to the same set
\(\{\,A^{\,s}B^{\,k}\mid k\in\mathbf Z_{31}\}\).

Therefore  
\[
|\mathrm{Sq}(G)|=15\times31=465. \tag{5}
\]

Explicitly,
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,2r}B^{\,k}\;\bigm|\;0\le r<15,\;0\le k<31\bigr\}. \tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------
c)  Counting the cubes
--------------------------------------------------
With the same notation,
\[
g^{3}=A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\,A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\,A^{\,i}B^{\,j}
       =A^{\,3i}\,B^{\,j\bigl(1+11^{\,i}+11^{\,2i}\bigr)}. \tag{7}
\]
Put  
\[
t(i)=3i\pmod{30},\qquad d(i)=1+11^{\,i}+11^{\,2i}\pmod{31}. \tag{8}
\]

Step 1.  Zeros of \(d(i)\).
Set \(x=11^{\,i}\).
Then \(d(i)=0\iff 1+x+x^{2}=0\iff x^{3}=1\) and \(x\neq1\).
In the cyclic group \((\mathbf Z_{31})^{\times}\) of order \(30\)
there are exactly two elements of order \(3\),
namely \(11^{10}\) and \(11^{20}\); hence
\[
d(i)=0\iff i\equiv10,20\pmod{30}. \tag{9}
\]

Step 2.  Non-zero \(d(i)\).
For the remaining \(28\) indices \(d(i)\neq0\); varying \(j\) runs through all \(31\) exponents of \(B\).

Step 3.  Identifying equal \(A\)-exponents.
The map \(i\mapsto t(i)=3i\) has kernel \(\{0,10,20\}\) and image the ten
residues \(0,3,6,\dots,27\).  Each residue occurs for three different
\(i\)'s, and at least one of them satisfies \(d(i)\neq0\).
Thus every coset \(\{\,A^{\,t}B^{\,k}\mid k\}\) with
\(t\equiv0,3,6,\dots,27\) appears exactly once.

Consequently  
\[
|\mathrm{Cu}(G)|=10\times31=310. \tag{10}
\]

Explicitly,
\[
\mathrm{Cu}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,3r}B^{\,k}\;\bigm|\;0\le r<10,\;0\le k<31\bigr\}. \tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------
d)  Squares that are also cubes
--------------------------------------------------
An element \(A^{\,\ell}B^{\,k}\) lies in \(\mathrm{Sq}(G)\) iff \(\ell\) is even,
and in \(\mathrm{Cu}(G)\) iff \(\ell\equiv0\pmod{3}\).
Therefore
\[
\ell\equiv0\pmod{2},\quad\ell\equiv0\pmod{3}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\ell\equiv0\pmod{6}.
\]
Within \(0\le\ell<30\) this gives
\[
\ell\in\{0,6,12,18,24\}. \tag{12}
\]
For each such \(\ell\) the descriptions (6) and (11) both provide every
exponent of \(B\), so for every \(k\) there exist elements whose square and whose cube equal \(A^{\,\ell}B^{\,k}\).
Hence
\[
\mathrm{Sq}(G)\cap\mathrm{Cu}(G)=
\bigl\{\,A^{\,\ell}B^{\,k}\;\bigm|\;
\ell\in\{0,6,12,18,24\},\;0\le k<31\bigr\}, \tag{13}
\]
and
\[
|\mathrm{Sq}(G)\cap\mathrm{Cu}(G)|=5\times31=155. \tag{14}
\]

--------------------------------------------------
Summary
--------------------------------------------------
1.  Unique normal form \(A^{\,i}B^{\,j}\) with \((i,j)\in[0,29]\times[0,30]\).  
2.  Number of squares:  \(|\mathrm{Sq}(G)|=465\).  
3.  Number of cubes:    \(|\mathrm{Cu}(G)|=310\).  
4.  Simultaneous squares and cubes: \(155\) elements, explicitly listed above.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-order relations.  The exponents 30 and 31 and the primitive-root
   conjugation A B A^{-1}=B^{11} force use of cyclic-group automorphisms
   of order 30, far beyond the inversion relation of the original problems.

2. Multiple interacting concepts.  Solving parts (b)–(d) requires
   simultaneous control of three ingredients:
   • semidirect-product normal forms,
   • arithmetic in (ℤ/31ℤ)^×, including primitive roots and roots of unity,
   • counting images of non-trivial endomorphisms of finite abelian groups.

3. Deeper theoretical tools.  One must exploit properties of cyclic groups,
   orders of automorphisms, geometric-series identities in group rings, and
   Chinese-remainder–type counting (e.g. lcm considerations to intersect the
   square and cube sets).

4. Substantially longer solution path.  Where the original problem needed
   only a single conjugation identity, this variant demands four main steps:
   establishing normal form, analysing squares, analysing cubes, and then
   intersecting the two large sets, each step involving non-trivial number-theoretic
   calculations.

5. Much larger search space.  The group now has 930 elements (vs. 66 or 132
   in the originals), and the answer sets (465, 310, 155 elements) cannot be
   listed by inspection; a structural approach is indispensable.

These additions create a kernel variant that is markedly more intricate and
conceptually demanding than both the original and the current kernel versions.
